title: Building a Reliable Podcast Production Pipeline
subtitle: From raw recording to publish-ready episode output
summary: Episode 14 explores how small podcast teams build a reliable production pipeline: capture, post-production, content extraction, review, and distribution. The conversation emphasizes clean transcripts, standard output bundles, careful verification of names and links, and using automation to accelerate drafts rather than approvals.
long_description: A good podcast pipeline is less about heroics and more about consistency. In Episode 14, the host and Maya Chen break down the five core stages of production — capture, post-production, content extraction, review and approval, and distribution — and explain why clean transcripts, standard naming, and a fixed output bundle matter for every release. The episode also covers how to separate raw and cleaned transcripts, generate useful title candidates, and keep humans responsible for what goes live.
short_description: How small teams build a reliable podcast production pipeline without depending on memory or heroics.
highlights: The pipeline is the product when publication quality depends on repeatable handoffs.
Keep raw and cleaned transcripts separate so downstream content starts from a cleaner source.
Use automation for drafts and packaging, but keep approvals human-owned.
Verify names, links, sponsor language, and metadata consistency before publish.

chapter_markers.title: Where automation helps and where it breaks

social_copy.post:
Episode 14 is out: Building a Reliable Podcast Production Pipeline. Maya Chen and I break down the five stages, why clean transcripts matter, and how to use automation for drafts without surrendering approvals.

Links: https://creators.example.com/checklists/podcast-release
social_copy.reply: The big takeaway: the pipeline is the product. Standardize the output bundle, verify names and links, and keep humans on approval.

release_notes: Grounded in the clean transcript only.
Verified links limited to the provided allowlist.
Treat publish/.keep as scaffolding, not release content.
